q: How do you query the DLT event log to track data quality expectation violation trends across pipeline runs?
a: Every Lakeflow Declarative Pipeline writes a hidden Delta event log table. For pipelines running in Unity Catalog, you access it with the event_log() table-valued function; for legacy pipelines you query the hidden table directly via a view you create at pipeline setup. The event log has columns: id, timestamp, event_type, details (JSON), origin (JSON containing pipeline_id, update_id, flow_name), and level. Data quality metrics live inside flow_progress events at details:flow_progress:data_quality:expectations — an array of structs with name, dataset, passed_records, and failed_records. To build a trend, explode that array, group by update_id + expectation name, and join origin.timestamp to get a time series. Alert when failed_records / (passed_records + failed_records) exceeds your SLA threshold. The mistake is relying only on the pipeline UI's Data Quality tab — it shows the last run, not history. Only event log queries give you week-over-week trends to catch slow degradation.
fj: I check the Data Quality tab in the pipeline UI after each run to see if expectations passed.
fs: The UI tab shows the latest run only. For trending, degradation detection, and SLA alerting you need to query the event log directly. Wire a Databricks SQL dashboard or alert against the event_log expectation metrics — that's the production monitoring pattern.

code.label: Create event log view (UC pipeline)
lines: CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW pipeline_event_log AS
